name: edge_bevel_open_end
description: Edge Bevel on a BOUNDARY edge of an OPEN mesh (only 1 adjacent face, not 2) -- the open-mesh end-cap case. A 2x1 open grid (6 verts, 2 quads sharing one interior edge); the selected edge is fully on the boundary (both endpoints valence-2 boundary corners, not touching the shared interior edge). Reference topology: 6v/2f -> 7v/2f, the touched quad becomes a pentagon, the untouched quad is unchanged. SURPRISING, VERIFIED-REPRODUCIBLE finding: the reference splits the 2 endpoints of the SAME boundary edge ASYMMETRICALLY -- one endpoint gets 2 new vertices, the other gets only 1 (net +1 vertex per boundary edge bevel, vs. the +2 a closed-mesh isolated edge gets, see edge_bevel_single.json). Confirmed on TWO independent open-mesh layouts (this 2-quad grid AND a degenerate single open quad, both gave the same 1-vs-2 split); the endpoint that comes SECOND in the bordering face's own winding-order traversal of the edge is the one that gets 2 replacements. This is real, reproducible reference behavior, not a capture artifact -- vibe3d's bevelEdgesByMask has no boundary-edge path at all today (its valence-3 guard requires a closed manifold vertex on BOTH endpoints, so a boundary edge is silently skipped/no-op), so this fixture is establishing brand-new target geometry, including this asymmetry, for the cap fix.
source: Frozen reference-editor capture via the private capture harness's edge_bevel op (task 0391), case edge_bevel_open_grid_w01.json (raw_mesh setup, 2x1 open grid), width=0.1.

provenance.harness: private capture harness (reference-editor edge-bevel tool + raw-mesh setup, driven headlessly, mode=inset)
provenance.notes: Cross-checked against a second, more degenerate open-mesh layout (a single open quad, edge_bevel_open_quad_w01.json capture, not committed as its own fixture) which reproduced the SAME asymmetric 1-vs-2 endpoint split -- rules out a single-face artifact. vibe3d has no boundary-edge bevel path today (valence-3-both-endpoints guard); this is new target geometry, not a regression lock.
